q: In a potentiometer arrangement, a cell gives a balancing point at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length of wire. This cell is now replaced by another cell of unknown emf. If the ratio of the emf's of two cells respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>, the difference in the balancing length of the potentiometer wire in above two cases will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: In a potentiometer arrangement, a cell gives a balancing point at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length of wire. This cell is now replaced by another cell of unknown emf. If the ratio of the emf's of two cells respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>, the difference in the balancing length of the potentiometer wire in above two cases will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
If the potential difference between the potentiometer is V and the potential of the battery is ε. 

Then, εV=lL

Therefore, ε1ε2=l1l2⇒32=75x⇒x=50 cm

Therefore, the difference in the balancing length is 75-50=25 cm

subject: Physics
q: Which one of the following is not a measurable quantity?
question: Which one of the following is not a measurable quantity?
solution:
In electrostatics and circuit theory, potential (voltage) at a single point is defined relative to a reference point (usually infinity or ground where potential is assumed to be zero).
Absolute voltage at a point is not a physically measurable quantity because only the difference in potential between two points can be measured using instruments like a voltmeter.
Resistance can be measured using an ohmmeter or bridge circuits.
Displacement current is a physical quantity that can be calculated and its effects (magnetic field) can be measured.
Voltage difference (potential difference) is the standard quantity measured between two points in a circuit.
Therefore, absolute voltage is not a measurable quantity.

q: Two resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mi>Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mi>Ω</mi></math> are connected in series with a battery of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and negligible internal resistance. A voltmeter is used to measure voltage across <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mi>Ω</mi></math> resistance, which gives reading as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The resistance of voltmeter must be _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ω</mi></math>.

A voltmeter is used to measure voltage across <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mi>Ω</mi></math> resistance, which gives reading as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The resistance of voltmeter must be _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ω</mi></math>.
solution:
Voltage across 200 Ω=4-1=3 V.

Therefore, current through the battery, 3200

Now, equivalent resistance of RV & 100 Ω,=Rv100Rv+100

For voltmeter, Rv100Rv+100×3200=1

⇒3Rv=2Rv+200

⇒Rv=200 Ω

q: The electric current through a wire varies with time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mi>t</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The amount of electric charge crossed through a section of the wire in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
question: The electric current through a wire varies with time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mi>t</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The amount of electric charge crossed through a section of the wire in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
solution:
Given that
Current I=I0+βt, I0=20 A and β=3 A s-1

The instantaneous current is given by

I=20+3t   ...1

Integrating equation (1), we have

dqdt=20+3t⇒∫0qdq=∫020(20+3t)dt⇒q=∫02020dt+∫0203tdt⇒q=20t+3t22020=1000 C

For designing a voltmeter of range 50 V and an ammeter of range 10 mA using a galvanometer which has a coil of resistance 54 Ω showing a full scale deflection for 1 mA as in figure.

(A) for voltmeter R≈50 kΩ

(B) for ammeter r≈0.2 Ω

(C) for ammeter r≈6 Ω

(D) for voltmeter R≈5 kΩ

(E) for voltmeter R≈500 Ω

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
For designing a voltmeter of range 50 V and an ammeter of range 10 mA using a galvanometer which has a coil of resistance 54 Ω showing a full scale deflection for 1 mA as in figure.

(A) for voltmeter R≈50 kΩ

(B) for ammeter r≈0.2 Ω

(C) for ammeter r≈6 Ω

(D) for voltmeter R≈5 kΩ

(E) for voltmeter R≈500 Ω

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The resistance is given by 

R=VIg-G

⇒5010-3-54≈50 kΩ

For ammeter,

S=IgGI-Ig⇒S=10-3×54(10-1)×10-3=6 Ω

subject: Physics
q: A wire of resistance 160 Ω is melted and drawn in a wire of one-fourth of its length. The new resistance of the wire will be
question: A wire of resistance 160 Ω is melted and drawn in a wire of one-fourth of its length. The new resistance of the wire will be
solution:
The formula to calculate the resistance of the original wire is given by

R=ρlA   ...1

When the new wire is formed, its resistance is given by

R'=ρl'4A   ...2

Divide equation (2) by equation (1) and simplify to obtain the expression for the new resistance.

R'R=ρl'4AρlA⇒R'=Rl'4l   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required new resistance.

R'=l44l×160 Ω= 10 Ω

subject: Physics
q: The length of a given cylindrical wire is increased to double of its original length. The percentage increase in the resistance of the wire will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math>
question: The length of a given cylindrical wire is increased to double of its original length. The percentage increase in the resistance of the wire will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math>
solution:
As volume is constant, so, length doubled and area reduces to half.

Resistance of a wire is given by R=ρlA, where, ρ is resistivity of a material.

Here, R1=ρlA and R2=ρ2lA2=4R

So percentage increase in the resistance will be =4R-RR×100%=300%

Then,
question: A moving coil galvanometer, having a resistance <math><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mo>,</mo></math> produces full scale deflection when a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi>G</mi></msub></math> flows through it. This galvanometer can be converted into (i) an ammeter of range 0 to <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> by connecting a shunt resistance <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow></msub></math> to it and (ii) into a voltmeter of range 0 to <math><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> by connecting a series resistance <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow></msub></math> to it. Then,
solution:
For ammeter: I0-IG×  RA=IG×G⇒I0=IG×G+RARA
For voltmeter: V0=IGG+RV
∵V0=I0×G
⇒IG×G+RV=IG×G+RA×GRA
⇒RA×G+RV×RA=G2+RA×G
⇒RVRA=G2
Again,   RARV=IG×GI0-IG×IGI0-IG×G=IG2I0-IG2

subject: Physics
q: Two equal resistances when connected in series to a battery, consume electric power of $60 \mathrm{~W}$. If these resistance are now connected in parallel combination to the same battery, the electric power consumed will be :
question: Two equal resistances when connected in series to a battery, consume electric power of $60 \mathrm{~W}$. If these resistance are now connected in parallel combination to the same battery, the electric power consumed will be :
solution:
When two resistances are connected in series, $R_{e q}=2 R$

Power consumed, $\mathrm{P}=\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{ea}}}=\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{2 \mathrm{R}}$

In parallel condition, $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathrm{R} / 2$ New power, $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}=\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{(\mathrm{R} / 2)}$

or $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}=4 \mathrm{P}=240 \mathrm{~W}(\because \mathrm{P}=60 \mathrm{~W})$

question:
To verify Ohm’s law, a student connects the voltmeter across the battery as shown in the figure. The measured voltage is plotted as a function of the current, and the following graph is obtained:

If Vo is almost zero, identify the correct statement:
options: The emf of the battery is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and its internal resistance is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>
The value of the resistance <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>

The potential difference across the battery is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> when it sends a current of <math><mn>1000</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>

The emf of the battery is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>
solution:
V=E-Ir
when V=V0=0⇒0=E-Ir
∴ E=Ir
when I=0, V=E=1.5 V
∴ r=1.5 Ω.

q: The current flowing through the $1 \Omega$ resistor is $\frac{n}{10}$ A. The value of $n$ is _________
question: The current flowing through the $1 \Omega$ resistor is $\frac{n}{10}$ A. The value of $n$ is _________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{y-5}{2}+\frac{y-0}{2}+\frac{y-x+10}{1}=0 \\ & y-5+y+2 y-2 x+20=0 \\ & 4 y-2 x+15=0 \quad \ldots .(\text { (i) } \\ & \frac{x-5}{4}+\frac{x-0}{4}+\frac{x-10-y}{1}=0 \\ & x-5+x+4 x-40-4 y=0 \\ & 6 x-4 y-45=0 \quad . .(i) \\ & \frac{-2 x+4 y+15=0 . .(i i)}{4 x-30=0} \\ & x=\frac{15}{2} \& 4 y-15+15=0 \\ & y=0 \\ & i=\frac{y-x+10}{1} \\ & i=\frac{0-7.5+10}{1} \\ & i=2.5 A=\frac{n}{10} A \\ & n=25\end{aligned}$

q: A current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mA</mi></math> was passed through an unknown resistor which dissipated a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4.4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Dissipated power when an ideal power supply of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is connected across it is:
question: A current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mA</mi></math> was passed through an unknown resistor which dissipated a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4.4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Dissipated power when an ideal power supply of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is connected across it is:
solution:
P=V2R=i2RResistance R=V2P=Pi2

Here,  Power P=4.4 W   and i=2 mA  

so R=4.44 ×10-6 Ω

When 11 V power supply is connected across R 

P′=1124.4×4×10-6

=11×10-5 W.

subject: Physics
q:
A. The drift velocity of electrons decreases with the increase in the temperature of conductor.
B. The drift velocity is inversely proportional to the area of cross-section of given conductor.
C. The drift velocity does not depend on the applied potential difference to the conductor.
D. The drift velocity of electron is inversely proportional to the length of the conductor.
E. The drift velocity increases with the increase in the temperature of conductor.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
A. The drift velocity of electrons decreases with the increase in the temperature of conductor.
B. The drift velocity is inversely proportional to the area of cross-section of given conductor.
C. The drift velocity does not depend on the applied potential difference to the conductor.
D. The drift velocity of electron is inversely proportional to the length of the conductor.
E. The drift velocity increases with the increase in the temperature of conductor.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Drift velocity =eτmE

vd=eτmΔVl

ΔV= Potential difference applied across the wire As temperature increases, relaxation time decreases, hence Vd decreases.
As per formula, Vd∝1l

vd=I neA , as it is not mentioned that current is at steady state neither it is mentioned that n is constant for given conductor. So it can't be said that vd is inversely proportional to A.

I=neAvd=VR=VρlA

vd=eEτm

τ decrease with temperature increase.

First and fourth statements are correct.

vd=Vρlne  E=Vl

subject: Physics
q:
Two electric bulbs marked $25 \mathrm{~W}-220 \mathrm{~V}$ and $100 \mathrm{~W}-220 \mathrm{~V}$ are connected in series to a $440 \mathrm{~V}$ supply. 
Which of the bulbs will fuse?
question:
Two electric bulbs marked $25 \mathrm{~W}-220 \mathrm{~V}$ and $100 \mathrm{~W}-220 \mathrm{~V}$ are connected in series to a $440 \mathrm{~V}$ supply. 
Which of the bulbs will fuse?
solution:
Resistances of both the bulbs are
$\mathrm{R}_1=\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{\mathrm{P}_1}=\frac{220^2}{25}$
$\mathrm{R}_2=\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{\mathrm{P}_2}=\frac{220^2}{100}$
Hence $R_1>R_2$
When connected in series, the voltages divide in them in the ratio of their resistances. The voltage of $440\mathrm{~V}$ devides in such a way that voltage across $25 \mathrm{~w}$ bulb will be more than $220 \mathrm{~V}$.
Hence $25 \mathrm{~w}$ bulb will fuse.

subject: Physics
q: A meter bridge setup is shown in the figure. It is used to determine an unknown resistance R using a given resistor of 15 Ω. The galvanometer G shows null defection when tapping key is at 43 cm mark from end A. If the end correction for end A is 2 cm, then the determined value of R will be Ω.
question: A meter bridge setup is shown in the figure. It is used to determine an unknown resistance R using a given resistor of 15 Ω. The galvanometer G shows null defection when tapping key is at 43 cm mark from end A. If the end correction for end A is 2 cm, then the determined value of R will be Ω.
solution:
Using the conditions of a balanced wheatstone bridge and adding the end correction.

15R=l+eL+e-l+e⇒1543+2=R102-45⇒R=5745×15

⇒R=19 Ω

subject: Physics
q: To measure the temperature coefficient of resistivity α of a semiconductor, an electrical arrangement shown in the figure is prepared. The arm BC is made up of the semiconductor. The experiment is being conducted at 25°C and resistance of the semiconductor arm is 3 mΩ. Arm BC is cooled at a constant rate of 2 °C s-1. If the galvanometer G shows no deflection after 10 s, then α is:
question: To measure the temperature coefficient of resistivity α of a semiconductor, an electrical arrangement shown in the figure is prepared. The arm BC is made up of the semiconductor. The experiment is being conducted at 25°C and resistance of the semiconductor arm is 3 mΩ. Arm BC is cooled at a constant rate of 2 °C s-1. If the galvanometer G shows no deflection after 10 s, then α is:
solution:
For no deflection(balanced condition) 0.81=R3

⇒R=2.4 mΩ

Temperature fall in 10 s = 20 °C

ΔR=RαΔt

⇒α=ΔRRΔt=2.4-33×20

=-1×10-2 °C-1

subject: Physics
q: Six equal resistances are connected between points $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ as shown in figure. Then net resistance will be maximum between :
question: Six equal resistances are connected between points $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ as shown in figure. Then net resistance will be maximum between :
solution:
Resistance between $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Q}$
$$
r_{\mathrm{PQ}}=r_{11}\left(\frac{r}{3}+\frac{r}{2}\right)=\frac{r \times \frac{5}{6} r}{r+\frac{5}{6} r}=\frac{5}{11} r
$$
Resistance between Q and R
$$
\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{QR}}=\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2} 11\left(\mathrm{r}+\frac{\mathrm{r}}{3}\right)=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2} \times \frac{4}{3} \mathrm{r}}{\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}+\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{r}}=\frac{4}{11} \mathrm{r}
$$
Resistance between $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{R}$
$$
\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{PR}}=\frac{\mathrm{r}}{3} \mathrm{l}\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}+\mathrm{r}\right)=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{r}}{3} \times \frac{3}{2} \mathrm{r}}{\frac{\mathrm{r}}{3}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{r}}=\frac{3}{11} \mathrm{r}
$$
Hence, it is clear that $\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{PQ}}$ is maximum

subject: Physics
q: An electric bulb rated as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used in a circuit having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply. The resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> that must be put in series with the bulb so that the bulb delivers the same power is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>.
question: An electric bulb rated as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used in a circuit having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply. The resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> that must be put in series with the bulb so that the bulb delivers the same power is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>.
solution:
Power, P=V2RB

RB=V2P=100×100200

RB=50 Ω

To produce same power, same voltage (i.e., 100 V) should be across the bulb. 

Hence, R=RB

R=50 Ω

subject: Physics
q:
It is preferable to measure the e.m.f. of a cell by potentiometer than by a voltmeter because of the following possible reasons.
(i) In case of potentiometer, no current flows through the cell.
(ii) The length of the potentiometer allows greater precision.
(iii) Measurement by the potentiometer is quicker.
(iv) The sensitivity of the galvanometer, when using a potentiometer is not relevant.
Which of these reasons are correct?
question:
It is preferable to measure the e.m.f. of a cell by potentiometer than by a voltmeter because of the following possible reasons.
(i) In case of potentiometer, no current flows through the cell.
(ii) The length of the potentiometer allows greater precision.
(iii) Measurement by the potentiometer is quicker.
(iv) The sensitivity of the galvanometer, when using a potentiometer is not relevant.
Which of these reasons are correct?
solution:
To measure the emf of a cell we prefer potentiometer rather than voltmeter because
(i) the length of potentiometer which allows greater precision.
(ii) in case of potentiometer, no current flows through the cell.
(iii) of high sensitivity.

question: An electrical power line, having a total resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>Ω</mi><mo>,</mo></math> delivers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The efficiency of the transmission line is approximately :
solution:
i=PV=1000220

PR=i2R

η=1000×1001000+41.32=96%

question: Sliding contact of a potentiometer is in the middle of the potentiometer wire having resistance $R_p=1 \Omega$ as shown in the figure. An external resistance of $R_e=2 \Omega$ is connected via the sliding contact. The electric current in the circuit is :

The circuit can be considered as
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{R}_{\text {eq }}=0.5+\frac{0.5 \times 2}{2+0.5}=\left(\frac{5}{10}+\frac{10}{25}\right) \Omega \\ & =\frac{45}{50}=\frac{9}{10}=0.9 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{i}=\frac{0.9}{0.9}=1 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

q:
The circuit shown here has two batteries of $8.0 \mathrm{~V}$ and $16.0 \mathrm{~V}$ and three resistors $3 \Omega, 9 \Omega$ and $9 \Omega$ and a capacitor of $5.0 \mu \mathrm{F}$.
How much is the current $\mathrm{I}$ in the circuit in steady state?
question:
The circuit shown here has two batteries of $8.0 \mathrm{~V}$ and $16.0 \mathrm{~V}$ and three resistors $3 \Omega, 9 \Omega$ and $9 \Omega$ and a capacitor of $5.0 \mu \mathrm{F}$.
How much is the current $\mathrm{I}$ in the circuit in steady state?
solution:
In steady state capacitor is fully charged hence no current will flow through line 2. By simplyfing the circuit
Hence resultant potential difference across resistances will be $8.0 \mathrm{~V}$. Thus current $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{8.0}{3+9}=\frac{8}{12}$ or, $\mathrm{I}=\frac{2}{3}=0.67 \mathrm{~A}$

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit the value of I1+I3I2 is:
question: In the given circuit the value of I1+I3I2 is:
solution:
Potential distribution at each point in the circuit is shown in above diagram.

Since, both 10 Ω resistors are maintained at same potential difference, so current will be same, thus, using Ohm's law, I1=I2=20-1010=1 A

Now, current I3=1010=1 A

Hence, the value of I1+I3I2=1+11=2.

subject: Physics
q: The length of a given cylindrical wire is increased by $100 \%$. Due to the consequent decrease in diameter the change in the resistance of the wire will be
question: The length of a given cylindrical wire is increased by $100 \%$. Due to the consequent decrease in diameter the change in the resistance of the wire will be
solution:
$\mathrm{L}_1=2 \mathrm{l}$ or $\left(\pi \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{l}\right)=\left(\pi \mathrm{r}_2^2\right)(2 \mathrm{l})$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_2=\frac{\mathrm{r}}{\sqrt{2}}$$;$               $\mathrm{R}=\rho \frac{1}{\pi^2}$
$\mathrm{R}_{\text {new }}=(\rho) \frac{21}{(\pi)(\mathrm{r} / \sqrt{2})^2}=\frac{(\rho) 4 \mathrm{l}}{(\pi) \mathrm{r}^2}=4 \times \mathrm{R}$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{R}=4 \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{R}=3 \mathrm{R}$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}} \%=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}} \times 100=300 \%$

subject: Physics
q: A generator has armature resistance of $0.1 \Omega$ and develops an induced emf of $120 \mathrm{~V}$ when driven at its rated speed. Its terminal voltage when a current of $50 \mathrm{~A}$ is being drawn is
question: A generator has armature resistance of $0.1 \Omega$ and develops an induced emf of $120 \mathrm{~V}$ when driven at its rated speed. Its terminal voltage when a current of $50 \mathrm{~A}$ is being drawn is
solution:
Armature resistance $R=0.1 \Omega$
Induced emf, $e=120 \mathrm{~V}$
Current drown, $I=50 \mathrm{~A}$
We know that,
$e=V+I R$
or, $V=e-I R=120-50 \times 0.1=115 \mathrm{~V}$

question: An electric bulb of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used in a circuit having a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply. solution:
Resistance of bulb

P=V2r

r=(100)2500=100×100500=20

i=PV=500100=5 A

ΔV in resistance R=100 V

R=1005=20 Ω

q: The electrochemical equivalent of a metal is $3.3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~kg}$ per coulomb. The mass of the metal liberated at the cathode when a 3 A current is passed for 2 seconds will be
question: The electrochemical equivalent of a metal is $3.3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~kg}$ per coulomb. The mass of the metal liberated at the cathode when a 3 A current is passed for 2 seconds will be
solution:
$\mathrm{m}=$ Zit,
$\mathrm{m}=3.3 \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 2=19.8 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~kg}$.
